answer:
### Topic: Dealing with an Unreliable Friend

Both *Maxims I* (ch. 6 of *Old English Elegies*) and *Ślokāntara* (ch. 1) offer gnomic counsel on friendship, betrayal, and the perils of unreliable companions, fitting their distilled-wisdom styles: the former through stark, proverbial warnings rooted in fate and social bonds; the latter via pragmatic maxims emphasizing discernment and action over mere relation.

#### From *Maxims I*
The text warns of betrayal's misery and urges vigilance, portraying unreliable "friends" as akin to wolves—treacherous beasts that devour the isolated wanderer:

> Blessed is he who thrives in his homeland, wretched is he whom his friends betray.[^1]  
> ...  
> A man should hold a friend well on every road; often a man travels far from his town, where he knows no certain friend. The friendless, unhappy man takes wolves for his companions, a very treacherous beast. Full oft that companion tears him apart; terror must be for the gray wolf, a grave for the dead man; it howls from hunger, it does not wrap the corpse in a shroud, nor indeed does the gray wolf weep for the slaughter, the violent death of men, but always wants more.[^2]

Counsel here: Cherish true friends (especially when far from home), for betrayal brings wretchedness, and isolation invites predatory false ones. Prevention via loyalty; no direct "what to do" post-betrayal, but implies resilience through wisdom and divine order.

#### From *Ślokāntara* (Śloka 51)
A pithier, relational reframe: Judge companions by utility, not blood or label—harmful kin are effectively strangers, helpful outsiders kin:

> Even a stranger, if helpful, is a kinsman; even a kinsman, if harmful, is a stranger. A disease born of the body is harmful; a forest herb that helps is a friend.[^3]

Commentary: "Even if a person is a stranger, if they give instruction for the well-being of the body, that one is called a kinsman. Even if a person is a kinsman, if they do not give well-being to the body, but constantly cause pain, that one is called a stranger." Illustrated via the *Rāmāyaṇa*: Sugrīva shuns treacherous kin Bāli but seeks helpful "strangers" Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa as "medicine."

Counsel: Sever harmful ties (treat as strangers); embrace beneficial ones regardless of origin. Action-oriented: Prioritize deeds over status.

#### Whose Counsel Travels Better?
*Ślokāntara*'s travels farther—its crisp utility cuts across cultures, urging proactive discernment over fatalistic lament. *Maxims I*'s vivid wolf-metaphor warns poetically but lingers in Anglo-Saxon communal bonds and wyrd (fate), less portable to individualistic or merit-based views. Both timeless, but the Javanese maxim equips for betrayal's aftermath; the Elegies elegizes its sting.[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [p-84cf09](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [p-84cf09](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^3]: *Ślokāntara*, ch. 1, [paragraphs p-bf093b to p-1c880b](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-bf093b).
[^4]: Whole-chapter citations: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6, [/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/); *Ślokāntara*, ch. 1, [/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/).
